_purpose: Courthouse Atlas for Stutsman County, ND. Westernmost county in the cluster, on the drift prairie. County seat at Jamestown on the James River. Record survival is good from county organization (1873). German-Russian and Norwegian settlement streams.
display_name: Stutsman County, North Dakota — Courthouse Atlas

previous_courthouses.location: Jamestown — wooden frame building near the Northern Pacific depot
previous_courthouses.fate: Replaced as the county grew. The military presence at Fort Seward (later Fort Cross) provided some administrative stability in the earliest years.

record_losses.cause: Drought-era farm abandonment and population loss
records_destroyed: some rural school district records from abandoned townships
scattered justice of the peace records from communities that ceased to exist
records_survived: probate files — all in the courthouse at Jamestown
record_losses.scope: minor for county records; significant for township and school records
record_losses.description: The 1930s drought devastated Stutsman County more severely than any of the valley counties. Hundreds of families abandoned their farms. Some rural school districts dissolved as the population base evaporated. School records, justice of the peace records, and other township-level documents from abandoned communities were sometimes lost when the buildings that housed them were dismantled or abandoned. However, all county-level records in the Jamestown courthouse survived the drought era intact.
record_losses.narrative_impact: Stutsman County's core genealogical records are complete. Researchers looking for school records or very local documentation from abandoned rural communities may find gaps — not because of fire or flood but because the communities themselves ceased to exist during the 1930s drought.
record_losses.source: State Historical Society of North Dakota. Guide to County Records. Bismarck: SHSND, 2003. Robinson, Elwyn B. History of North Dakota. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 1966.

deeds.completeness: Good — continuous from county organization. Northern Pacific land-grant deeds, homestead patents, and private transactions all recorded.
deeds.gaps: No significant gaps. Some homestead entries that were abandoned (never proved up) appear in BLM records but not in county deed books.
deeds.repository: Stutsman County Recorder's Office, Jamestown; SHSND

wills_estates.gaps: Very few estates filed before 1885. Probate increases significantly after 1900 as first-generation settlers aged.
wills_estates.repository: Stutsman County Clerk of Court; SHSND

marriages.completeness: Good from county organization. Both Norwegian Lutheran and German-Russian church marriages sometimes preceded civil licensing.
marriages.gaps: Church marriages without civil license in the earliest settlement period.

court_minutes.completeness: Good from county organization. Fort Seward military records supplement civil court records for the earliest period.
court_minutes.gaps: Minor gaps in early territorial proceedings.
court_minutes.repository: Stutsman County Clerk of Court; SHSND

vital_records.completeness: Spotty until approximately 1910, especially in remote rural townships. Jamestown city records may be more complete than rural records for 1893-1910.
vital_records.repository: ND Department of Health Vital Records; SHSND

substitute_sources.description: Norwegian Lutheran congregations in Stutsman County maintained ministerialbok records in Norwegian.
substitute_sources.repository: NAHA, Northfield, MN; ELCA Archives; individual congregations; SHSND
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for Norwegian immigrant families. Often the only vital record before 1893.
substitute_sources.source: Lovoll, Odd S. The Promise of America. Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 1984.

substitute_sources.description: German-Russian settlements in Stutsman County maintained church records in German — Evangelical, Congregational, and Catholic denominations. Records connect families to villages of origin in the Black Sea or Volga regions of Russia.
substitute_sources.repository: Germans from Russia Heritage Collection, NDSU Libraries, Fargo; individual congregations; Archdiocese of Fargo Archives
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Critical for German-Russian families. Identifies village of origin in Russia — the key to accessing Russian-era church records and connecting to extended family networks.
substitute_sources.source: Sallet, Richard. Russian-German Settlements in the United States. Fargo: NDIRS, 1974.

substitute_sources.description: Homestead application files with detailed personal information.
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: Essential for settlers. The proof-up testimony provides a detailed snapshot of the homesteader's improvements and neighbors. Abandoned homestead entries — claims that were never proved up — are documented in BLM records and tell the story of settlers who failed.
substitute_sources.source: National Archives and Records Administration. Homestead Records.

substitute_sources.description: The North Dakota State Hospital at Jamestown (est. 1885) housed patients from across the state. Admission records include name, age, birthplace, and sometimes family information.
substitute_sources.repository: SHSND; ND State Hospital administrative records (restricted access for recent records)
substitute_sources.genealogical_value: If an ancestor was committed to the state hospital, the admission records may document a family member who otherwise disappeared from the civil record. Access restrictions apply to recent records.

narrative_hooks: Stutsman County's courthouse records survived the drought that broke everything else — the deeds, the marriages, the probate files are all in the vault at Jamestown, even for families whose farms turned to dust in the 1930s.

If {ancestor_name} abandoned a homestead in Stutsman County during the drought, the BLM records at the National Archives may document the claim that was never proved up — a record of failure that is also a record of effort.

The school where {ancestor_name}'s children learned to read may have no surviving records — not because of fire, but because the school district dissolved when the families left during the 1930s and the building was dismantled board by board.
{ancestor_name}'s German-Russian church record in Stutsman County may name the village in Russia that the family left behind — the thread connecting the drift prairie to the Black Sea steppe.
source_references: State Historical Society of North Dakota. Guide to County Records. Bismarck: SHSND, 2003.
Robinson, Elwyn B. History of North Dakota. Lincoln: University of Nebraska Press, 1966.
Lovoll, Odd S. The Promise of America: A History of the Norwegian-American People. Minneapolis: University of Minnesota Press, 1984.
Sallet, Richard. Russian-German Settlements in the United States. Fargo: North Dakota Institute for Regional Studies, 1974.
National Archives and Records Administration. Homestead Records at the National Archives. Washington, DC.
